intro_text:
Hat Kata is a roughly 1.5-kilometre crescent of pale, quartz-rich sand on the west coast of Phuket, facing the Andaman Sea about 17 km southwest of Phuket Town and 3 km south of Ban Karon. It is really two beaches sharing one bay: the longer northern half is Hat Kata Yai, and a low rocky headland separates it from the smaller, quieter Hat Kata Noi about a kilometre south. A small island, Koh Pu — 'Crab Island' — sits off the northern end of Kata Yai, close enough that strong swimmers reach it in calm months to snorkel the coral around it.

The thing to understand about Kata is that it faces west, into the weather. From roughly November to April the Andaman is flat and the bay is a warm, swimmable pool. Then the southwest monsoon arrives — May to October — and the same exposure becomes a wind machine: swell builds, the lifeguards plant red flags, and Kata turns from a family swimming beach into Phuket's best-known surf break. Our wave record puts the calmest month near 0.49 m (April) and the biggest monthly mean at 1.34 m (September) — the whole personality of the place in two numbers.

Kata's modern history starts with one building. In 1985 Club Med opened on Kata Yai — the first big resort on the beach — designed by the Thai architect Mom Luang Tridosyuth Devakul (Mom Tri), and is widely credited with helping put Phuket on the international map. Because it held a large single block of beachfront, the front of Kata Yai stayed comparatively green and low-rise rather than filling wall-to-wall with shophouses — part of why Kata still reads gentler than Patong to the north.

The honest version is not gentle. The monsoon that makes the surf is the rip-current season that kills swimmers nearly every year — in August 2025 a 10-year-old Canadian girl drowned at Kata on the second day of her family's holiday, one of several west-coast deaths that month. In 2014 the military junta (the NCPO) cleared the sunbeds and vendors off Kata and every Phuket beach in an anti-mafia purge, then capped vendor occupation at 10% of the sand — putting hundreds out of work. And Kata is one of the beaches where Phuket's jet-ski damage-deposit scam runs. All of that is the same bay as the September surf contest and the Andaman sunset — one half of the year swimmable and glassy, the other the surf and the drowning reports, the same west-facing bay under two opposite winds.
spike_explainer:
Most beaches have a best season and a worst season. Kata's singularity is that its best and its worst are the *same* season, driven by the *same* wind, and the line between them is the difference between a surfboard and a body bag. To understand Kata you have to understand that it faces west, into the Andaman, and that the Andaman runs on a monsoon switch.

**Start with the exposure.** Hat Kata is a west-facing crescent of about 1.5 km on Phuket's leeward-of-nothing Andaman coast. There is no reef a kilometre out to absorb the swell the way there is at, say, Diani in Kenya; the bay takes the ocean's energy more or less directly, softened only by its own curve and by the headland and Koh Pu island at the northern end. For roughly half the year that exposure does nothing dramatic. From about November to April the northeast monsoon sits offshore or light, the Andaman goes flat, and Kata is a wide warm swimming bay — our wave record bottoms out around 0.49 m in April, which is pool-calm. Families wade, longtail boats run snorkel trips out to Koh Pu, and the beach behaves exactly like the postcard.

**Then the wind reverses, and everything Kata is changes.** From roughly May to October the southwest monsoon blows in off the Indian Ocean. It is the rainy season — our rainfall record peaks hard in August through October, over 350 mm in August alone — but more importantly it is the *swell* season. The monsoon winds far out in the Andaman build a swell that marches straight into Kata's west-facing bay. Our wave data tracks the switch precisely: the monthly mean wave height climbs from sub-half-metre in April to about 0.88 m in May, 1.19 m in June, and peaks around 1.34 m in September, with individual big days near 2.3 m. By Phuket standards that is real surf, and Kata Yai is the island's most reliable, most accessible break — a beach break you can paddle out to from the sand, with surf schools renting boards along the front. The best-known conditions come when a southwest swell meets an offshore northeast wind, cleaning up the faces.

**That swell is why Kata has a surfing calendar at all.** The Phuket Surfing Contest is held annually at Kata, typically in the peak-swell window of August–September; Wikipedia records it on the September date. There is genuine surf-contest lineage here: a Quiksilver-branded Kata contest ran around 1999–2003, Kata hosted a leg of the first internationally-sanctioned Quiksilver Thailand Surf Series in 2009, and the locally-run Kata Beach Surfing Contest reached its 17th edition in 2018 — described as Phuket's longest-running surf competition. The surf is not a marketing invention bolted onto a swimming beach; it is a decades-old seasonal fixture built on the monsoon swell.

**And here is the spike, the uncomfortable hinge.** The exact swell that makes the September contest is the exact swell that drowns people. The southwest monsoon doesn't just stand surf up; it generates strong, fast rip currents along the bay, and the same west-facing exposure that has no reef to break the waves also has nothing to break the rips. Phuket records roughly 30–40 drowning deaths a year, concentrated in monsoon season, and Kata is repeatedly named among the worst. Authorities plant red flags written in Thai, English, Chinese and Russian; tourists who have just flown in from a flat-sea winter at home see warm tropical water, ignore the flag, and get pulled out — most drownings happen within the first few minutes of being caught. In August 2025 a 10-year-old Canadian girl drowned at Kata on the second day of her family's holiday, one of several Phuket west-coast deaths that month. The problem is compounded by Phuket's chronic lifeguard instability: in 2017 the long-running Phuket Lifeguard Service contract collapsed after a budget cut (from 22 million to a proposed 19 million baht), beaches were left thinly covered by stand-in government and naval staff, and drownings spiked during the gap. So the surfer and the swimmer are reading the same water and getting opposite answers from it: to the surfer the monsoon swell is the whole point of the year; to the swimmer it is the thing the red flag is trying to keep them out of.

**This is also why Kata's two halves matter.** Kata Yai, the longer northern beach, is the surf beach, the busy beach, the one with the break and the schools and the rips. Kata Noi, smaller and tucked behind its headland a kilometre south, is quieter, with fewer hotels — it is dominated at the back by the sprawling Kata Thani resort and feels more sheltered and serene. The bay's gentler reputation rests partly on a single 1985 decision: when Club Med opened on Kata Yai, designed by Mom Tri, it occupied a large block of beachfront and direct commercial wall-to-wall development never filled the front of the bay the way it did at Patong. That is why Kata can still look green from the Karon Viewpoint up the hill — the lookout the locals first called the Kata Viewpoint, where you can see Kata Noi directly below, Kata and Koh Pu next, and Karon's long pale curve in the distance, three beaches in one frame. But the green front and the gentle reputation don't change the water's physics. Kata is a beach with a switch, and the same hand that flips it to 'surf' flips it to 'drowning'.
honest_reckoning_note: Kata is sold as a soft, family-friendly alternative to Patong, and for half the year it is. The other half it kills people. The southwest monsoon (roughly May–October) that builds the surf also builds fast rip currents on a west-facing bay with no protecting reef, and Phuket records around 30–40 drownings a year, with Kata named repeatedly among the worst. Officials plant red flags in four languages; tourists fresh off a flight see warm water and walk past them, and most drownings happen within minutes of being caught. In August 2025 a 10-year-old Canadian girl drowned at Kata on the second day of her family's holiday. The danger is worsened by Phuket's lifeguard instability — in 2017 the long-running Phuket Lifeguard Service contract collapsed over a budget cut and drownings spiked while stand-in staff filled gaps. On land, Kata's tidy look is itself the product of a heavy hand: in 2014 the military junta (NCPO) cleared sunbeds and vendors off Kata and every Phuket beach in an anti-mafia sweep and capped vendor occupation at 10% of the sand, which decluttered the beach and put hundreds of people out of work — one Phuket police chief noted crime rose afterward as displaced vendors lost their income. And Kata is one of the beaches where the long-documented Phuket jet-ski 'damage deposit' scam runs, with operators claiming pre-existing damage and demanding thousands of baht in cash. None of this cancels Kata. It just means the gentle-family-beach story is a dry-season story told about a beach that has another season.
reckoning_pullquote: The same southwest swell that fills the September surf contest is the rip-current season that drowns swimmers who walk past a red flag printed in four languages.

key_facts.label: Length and shape
key_facts.value: About 1.5 km of west-facing Andaman-coast sand, split into Hat Kata Yai (longer, northern) and Hat Kata Noi (smaller, southern), divided by a low headland

key_facts.label: Location
key_facts.value: West coast of Phuket, ~17 km southwest of Phuket Town, 3 km south of Ban Karon; nearest airport Phuket International (HKT), ~32.6 km
key_facts.source: Wikipedia: Hat Kata; WorldBeachTour beach data

key_facts.label: Koh Pu (Crab Island)
key_facts.value: A small islet off the northern end of Kata Yai; strong swimmers reach it in the calm season to snorkel its coral

key_facts.label: The season switch (waves)
key_facts.value: Calmest ~0.49 m mean in April (dry season); biggest ~1.34 m mean in September (SW monsoon), with big days near 2.3 m — the same swell that surfs and that rips

key_facts.label: Surf season and contest
key_facts.value: SW monsoon ~May–Oct builds Phuket's most reliable surf; the Phuket Surfing Contest is held at Kata annually, typically Aug–Sep (Wikipedia: September)
key_facts.source: Wikipedia: Hat Kata; The Phuket News; Thailand Surfing Federation

key_facts.label: Drowning toll
key_facts.value: Phuket records ~30–40 drownings a year, concentrated in monsoon season, with Kata repeatedly among the worst; in Aug 2025 a 10-year-old Canadian girl drowned at Kata
key_facts.source: Khaosod English; Thai Examiner; Travel And Tour World

key_facts.label: Club Med, 1985
key_facts.value: Club Med opened on Kata Yai in 1985 — the first big resort on the beach, designed by Thai architect Mom Tri (M.L. Tridosyuth Devakul); credited with helping launch Phuket as an international destination
key_facts.source: Oyster.com; Club Med; true-beachfront.com photo history

key_facts.label: Tides
key_facts.value: Semidiurnal; spring range ~2.0 m, neap range ~0.67 m (EOT20 tidal model)

margin_notes.text: Kata's sand is quartz-dominant (~80% quartz by the GloPrSM model), not coral — pale and fine. It's one continuous 1.5 km bay split by a low rocky headland into Kata Yai (north) and Kata Noi (south), with Koh Pu island off the north end.

The whole beach runs on a season switch. April mean wave ~0.49 m (flat, swimmable); September mean ~1.34 m with big days near 2.3 m. The SW monsoon (May–Oct) is the surf season AND the rip-current season — same swell, opposite verdict.

Club Med opened on Kata Yai in 1985 (architect Mom Tri / M.L. Tridosyuth Devakul), the first big resort here. It sat on a large single block of beachfront, which is part of why Kata Yai's front stayed greener and lower-rise than Patong's.

Phuket records ~30–40 drownings/year, mostly in monsoon. Red flags are printed in Thai, English, Chinese and Russian; most drownings happen within minutes of being caught in a rip. In Aug 2025 a 10-year-old Canadian girl drowned at Kata on her family's second day.

Kata's tidy sand is partly the 2014 NCPO (junta) anti-mafia sweep: sunbeds and vendors cleared from every Phuket beach, vendor occupation later capped at 10% of the sand. It decluttered the beach and put hundreds of people out of work; crime reportedly rose as vendors lost income.

From the hilltop Karon Viewpoint (locals first called it the Kata Viewpoint) you see three beaches at once: Kata Noi directly below, Kata Yai with Koh Pu next, and Karon's long curve beyond. Free, with a parking area, best in morning or late-afternoon light.

day_in_time.dawn: At first light in the dry season the bay is glassy and the longtail boats are still moored, their engines cold; in the monsoon months dawn is when the surf is cleanest, before the onshore wind builds, and the surfers are already out off Kata Yai. Common mynas and zebra doves work the treeline behind the sand.
day_in_time.midday: Dry season: a wide warm swimming bay, families wading, snorkel longtails running out to Koh Pu. Monsoon season: red flags up, the surf stood tall by the southwest swell, the rip currents at their most dangerous — and the quartz sand bright and hot under a near-equatorial sun between rain squalls.
day_in_time.golden: Kata faces west, so the late-afternoon Andaman sun goes down straight into the bay — the one thing the monsoon and dry seasons share. Surfers come in, the light turns amber on pale sand and the green headlands, and the beach-front restaurants behind the sand fill for sunset.
day_in_time.night: After dark the action moves off the sand to the road behind it — bars and restaurants through Kata and up toward Karon. The beach itself is unlit and, in monsoon season, the surf is a line of white noise in the dark; this is also when the jet-ski and vendor friction is least visible and least supervised.

food_drink.name: Andaman seafood — grilled fish, prawns, squid
food_drink.description: Phuket's west coast lives on Andaman seafood: whole grilled fish, prawns, crab and squid, often done Thai-style with chilli, lime, garlic and fish sauce, or steamed with lime. Served at the restaurants lining the road behind Kata rather than on the open sand, where vendor stalls are now capped at a fraction of the beach.
food_drink.where: Beach-road restaurants behind Kata Yai and through to Karon

food_drink.name: Phuket-style Hokkien noodles and southern Thai curries
food_drink.description: Phuket has its own Peranakan-influenced cooking — Hokkien-style stir-fried noodles (mee Hokkien), and fierce southern-Thai curries like gaeng tai pla and yellow crab curry — distinct from central Thai food. The cheaper, more authentic versions are inland in Kata/Karon's local sois and in Phuket Town, not on the tourist strip.
food_drink.where: Local eateries in Kata, Ban Karon and Phuket Town

food_drink.name: Roti and fresh fruit from beach-road vendors
food_drink.description: Banana-and-egg roti cooked to order, and cut tropical fruit (mango, pineapple, watermelon) from carts, are the everyday street snacks along the Kata road. Buying from beach vendors is also the contact point with the workers most affected by the 2014 vendor clearances and the 10% sand-occupation cap.
food_drink.where: Roadside carts along Kata Beach Road

timeline.title: Club Med opens on Kata Yai — the first big resort
timeline.description: Club Med opened on Kata Yai in 1985, the first major resort on the beach, designed by Thai architect Mom Luang Tridosyuth Devakul (Mom Tri). It is widely credited with helping launch Phuket as an international beach destination; Thai Airways direct Bangkok–Phuket service followed. Sitting on a large single block of beachfront, it is part of why Kata Yai's front stayed greener than Patong's.
timeline.source: Oyster.com; Club Med; true-beachfront.com photo history

timeline.title: Quiksilver Kata Beach surf contest era begins
timeline.description: A Quiksilver-branded surfing contest ran at Kata Beach around 1999–2003, an early sign of Kata's emergence as Phuket's main monsoon-season surf break.

timeline.title: Kata hosts a leg of the first sanctioned Thailand surf series
timeline.description: Kata Beach hosted a competition in the inaugural Quiksilver Thailand Surf Series in 2009 — described as the first internationally brand-sponsored, ASC-sanctioned surf competition series in Thailand.
timeline.source: Thailand Surfing Federation; Asian Surf Co

timeline.title: Military junta (NCPO) clears Phuket's beaches
timeline.description: After the May 2014 coup, the National Council for Peace and Order launched an island-wide anti-mafia clean-up from July 2014, clearing sunbeds and vendors from Kata and every other Phuket beach and demolishing illegal beach structures. Around 500 vendors island-wide were affected.

timeline.title: Vendor occupation capped at 10% of the sand
timeline.description: In 2015, under NCPO authority, the Phuket provincial government mandated that beach vendors could occupy no more than 10% of any beach's sand area — institutionalising the cleared, low-clutter look of Kata and other beaches, and permanently shrinking the vendor economy.

timeline.title: Phuket Lifeguard Service contract collapses
timeline.description: In October 2017 the long-running Phuket Lifeguard Service contract ended after a budget cut (from 22 million baht toward a proposed 19 million), leaving beaches including Kata and Karon thinly covered by stand-in government and naval staff. Drownings spiked during the gap; a recruitment drive for new lifeguards followed.
timeline.source: Khaosod English; The Phuket News; Bangkok Post

timeline.title: 17th Kata Beach Surfing Contest
timeline.description: The locally-run Kata Beach Surfing Contest reached its 17th edition in 2018, described as Phuket's longest-running surf competition — confirming Kata as the island's established surf venue during the monsoon swell window.

timeline.title: 10-year-old Canadian girl drowns at Kata
timeline.description: In August 2025 a 10-year-old Canadian girl drowned at Kata Beach on the second day of her family's holiday, one of several monsoon-season drownings reported along Phuket's west coast that month, prompting renewed red-flag warnings in four languages.

zones.name: Hat Kata Yai (north)
zones.character: The longer, busier northern beach and Phuket's main surf break, with Koh Pu island off its northern tip. Surf schools and board rentals work the front in monsoon season; the swimming bay in the dry season. Most of Kata's beach-road life sits behind it.
zones.best_for: Surfing (May–Oct), dry-season swimming, longtail trips to Koh Pu
zones.notes: Strongest rip currents on the bay in monsoon season — obey the red flags. This is where most Kata drownings occur.

zones.name: Central headland / Club Med frontage
zones.character: The low rocky headland that divides Kata Yai from Kata Noi, with the large green Club Med block of beachfront behind it. Part of why the centre of the bay reads low-rise and planted rather than wall-to-wall built.
zones.best_for: Walking between the two beaches, shade and greenery
zones.notes: The resort frontage is private; the sand itself is public.

zones.name: Hat Kata Noi (south)
zones.character: The smaller, quieter southern beach about a kilometre south, with fewer hotels and a more sheltered, serene feel; the back of the beach is dominated by the sprawling Kata Thani resort. Calmer crowd than Kata Yai.
zones.best_for: Quieter swimming and sunbathing, a more secluded feel
zones.notes: Still a west-facing Andaman beach — monsoon rips apply here too; watch the flags.

landmarks.name: Koh Pu (Crab Island)
landmarks.description: A small island off the northern end of Kata Yai, called 'Crab Island' in English. In the calm dry season strong swimmers reach it from the beach to snorkel the coral around it; it frames nearly every photograph taken from the northern bay and from the Karon Viewpoint.

landmarks.name: Club Med Phuket
landmarks.description: The first big resort on Kata, opened on Kata Yai in 1985 and designed by the Thai architect Mom Tri, drawing on traditional Thai longhouse forms. Widely credited with helping launch Phuket as an international destination; its large block of beachfront is part of why the front of Kata Yai stayed green and low-rise.

landmarks.name: Karon Viewpoint (Three Beaches)
landmarks.description: A free hilltop lookout on the coastal road south of Kata Noi, originally called the Kata Viewpoint, from which three beaches are visible at once: Kata Noi directly below, Kata Yai with Koh Pu next, and Karon's long curve beyond. Has a shade gazebo, snack stalls and a large parking area; best light is morning or late afternoon.

cultural_refs.title: Phuket Surfing Contest at Kata
cultural_refs.creator: Phuket Surfing Association
cultural_refs.description: An annual surf competition held at Kata Beach during the southwest-monsoon swell, typically August–September (Wikipedia records it in September). Kata's surf-contest lineage runs through the Quiksilver-branded contests of ~1999–2003 and the locally-run Kata Beach Surfing Contest, which reached its 17th edition in 2018.

cultural_refs.title: Club Med Phuket (Mom Tri design, 1985)
cultural_refs.creator: Mom Luang Tridosyuth Devakul (Mom Tri)
cultural_refs.description: The first big resort on Kata, opened 1985, designed by the Thai architect-aristocrat Mom Tri in a style drawing on traditional Thai longhouses. A landmark in Phuket's transformation into an international tourist island.

cultural_refs.title: Big Trouble in Tourist Thailand (2009)
cultural_refs.description: A British TV documentary that brought international attention to Phuket's jet-ski 'damage' intimidation — capturing a Patong jet-ski operator threatening British marines over a disputed damage claim. The same jet-ski damage-deposit scam runs at Kata and other Phuket beaches.

cultural_refs.title: NCPO Phuket beach clean-up (2014)
cultural_refs.creator: National Council for Peace and Order
cultural_refs.description: The military junta's 2014 island-wide anti-mafia sweep cleared sunbeds and vendors from Kata and every Phuket beach and demolished illegal beach structures — the policy that produced Kata's cleared, low-clutter modern look and the 10%-of-sand vendor cap.

recurring_events.name: Phuket / Kata Beach surf contest
recurring_events.when_text: Annually in the peak-swell window, typically August–September
recurring_events.description: Kata's annual surf competition, run on the southwest-monsoon swell, draws boardriders from across Southeast Asia. The locally-run edition reached its 17th in 2018; Wikipedia records the Phuket Surfing Contest at Kata in September.
recurring_events.typical_attendance: 100+ competing surfers in recent international editions

recurring_events.name: Southwest-monsoon surf season
recurring_events.description: The SW monsoon builds Kata's surf, peaking around August–September (monthly mean wave ~1.34 m in September, big days near 2.3 m). It is simultaneously the rip-current and drowning season — the same swell, with red flags up.

recurring_events.name: Dry-season swimming window
recurring_events.description: When the monsoon drops, the Andaman flattens (April mean wave ~0.49 m) and Kata becomes a warm, swimmable bay with calm enough water for snorkel trips out to Koh Pu. This is the postcard half of the year.

businesses.name: Club Med Phuket
businesses.description: All-inclusive resort on Hat Kata Yai, opened 1985 as the first big resort on the beach; designed by Thai architect Mom Tri. Occupies a large block of Kata Yai beachfront. A verifiable landmark rather than a recommendation.
businesses.address: Kata Yai, Karon, Mueang Phuket District, Phuket, Thailand

businesses.name: Kata Thani Phuket Beach Resort
businesses.description: The large resort that dominates the back of Hat Kata Noi, the smaller southern beach. Frequently cited as the defining built feature of Kata Noi's beachfront.
businesses.address: Hat Kata Noi, Karon, Mueang Phuket District, Phuket, Thailand

things_to_know.label: Obey the red flags — this beach drowns people
things_to_know.note: In monsoon season (May–Oct) Kata's southwest swell builds strong rip currents. Phuket records ~30–40 drownings a year and Kata is among the worst; most happen within minutes of being caught. Red flags are printed in Thai, English, Chinese and Russian — a red flag means do not enter, no matter how warm or calm the water looks.

things_to_know.label: Two beaches, two moods
things_to_know.note: Kata Yai (north) is the longer, busier surf-and-swim beach with Koh Pu island off its tip; Kata Noi (south), a kilometre on past the headland, is smaller, quieter and more sheltered. Both face west and both get monsoon rips.

things_to_know.label: The jet-ski 'damage' scam is real here
things_to_know.note: Kata is one of the Phuket beaches where jet-ski operators may claim pre-existing damage on return and demand large cash sums (commonly thousands of baht), sometimes aggressively. Photograph and video any rental thoroughly before riding, or skip jet-skis entirely.

things_to_know.label: Sand vendors are now capped
things_to_know.note: Since the 2014 junta clean-up and the 2015 rule, beach vendors can occupy no more than 10% of the sand. Expect a tidy, lower-clutter beach than older photos show — sunbeds and stalls are restricted, and most food and shops are on the road behind the beach.

access_note: Most visitors fly into Phuket International Airport (HKT), about 32–33 km north of Kata; from the airport it is roughly 45 minutes to an hour by taxi, private transfer or the metered airport bus/minivan toward the southern beaches. Kata sits about 3 km south of Ban Karon and around 17 km southwest of Phuket Town, reached on the west-coast road that links Patong, Karon and Kata; local songthaews (converted pickups) run between Phuket Town and Kata in the daytime, and taxis and ride apps cover the rest. The Karon Viewpoint, with its three-beach panorama, is a short drive south of Kata Noi on the coastal road toward Nai Harn. The beach itself is public and free to access.
